strategy_update: Implement a two-phase approach: (1) Respect hard constraints strictly and reserve core period assignments for constrained exam pairs (E1-E2 same period, E5 before E6, E3-E4 different periods). (2) Separate high-overlap exam clusters by identifying exam groups sharing students (CS curriculum: E1,E2,E3,E5; Physics: E5,E6,E3,E4; etc.) and assigning them to non-adjacent periods. (3) Prioritize large exams (E8, E18, E19, E17, E3, E4) for early assignment to appropriate rooms, spreading them across all 8 periods rather than clustering. (4) Fill remaining periods with smaller exams that have minimal overlap.

strategy_update: 1) Enforce hard constraints absolutely: C1 (E1, E2 together in P1), C2 (E5 period < E6 period), C3 (E3 ≠ E4). 2) Spread remaining 18 exams across periods 2-8 to maximize separation of high-conflict pairs. 3) Match exam sizes to room capacities: large exams (40+ students) → R3/R5 (80/100), medium (25-39) → R1/R3, small (≤24) → R1/R2/R4. 4) Place exams with highest degree conflicts in non-adjacent periods when possible.

strategy_update: Implement strict priority ordering: (1) Identify complete student-conflict graph—exams sharing ≥1 student cannot coexist in same period; (2) Apply graph coloring with conflict-degree ordering: prioritize exams with most conflicts (E3, E4, E11, E7, E2) and assign to earliest available periods that avoid ALL conflicting pairs; (3) Enforce hard availability constraints (E13 excluded from period 4, E5 before E6 per precedence) before placing exams; (4) Assign rooms respecting both available-room constraints and capacity; (5) Accept that C1 (E1, E2 same period) is infeasible and will be violated; (6) Verify zero hard-constraint violations before accepting solution.

strategy_update: Distribute exams evenly across available periods (use periods 1-10 rather than 1-2). Identify exam pairs that share many students and assign them to different periods. Respect hard constraints: E1+E2 must be same period (even if they share students, this is a linked course), E3 and E4 must differ, E5 before E6. Assign exams from different departments (English, CS, Math, Physics, Chemistry, etc.) to the same period when their room assignments don't conflict.

strategy_update: Implemented a capacity-aware matching strategy: (1) Pre-matched exams to rooms based on student count before period assignment, ensuring no overflow; (2) Prioritized hard constraints first (C1, C2, C3) by locking E10+E20 together, E5 before E6, and E3 separate from E4; (3) Spread exams across periods 1-8 using room diversity per period to avoid clustering; (4) Verified each assignment against capacity limits before inclusion.

strategy_update: New strategy: (1) Enforce all hard distribution constraints first—place E10 and E20 together, ensure E5 before E6, separate E3 and E4. (2) Use a greedy round-robin spread across 8 periods to minimize student conflicts. (3) Assign rooms carefully by exam size: use R3 (80) and R5 (100) for large exams, R1 (60) for medium, and R2/R4 for small exams. (4) Ensure each room hosts at most one exam per period. (5) Avoid penalty periods 9-10 when possible.

strategy_update: Spread all 20 exams across periods 1-8 evenly. Enforce hard constraints first: (1) E1+E2 in same period per C1, (2) E5 before E6 per C2, (3) E3 != E4 per C3. Separate high-conflict exam clusters by curriculum: CS exams (E1,E2,E9,E10,E15,E16) across different periods; MATH exams (E3,E4,E11,E14) spread; Physics/CHEM (E5,E6,E8,E12,E13,E17) distributed; Social sciences (E18,E19,E20) in non-adjacent periods. Assign rooms respecting availability: E18 (60 students) must use R5 only; large exams (40+) to R3/R5; medium exams to R1/R2/R3; small exams to R1/R2/R4.
